15. What is difference between CAT5 and CAT6 cable? 
 
 
  
The big difference between Cat5 and Cat6 cables is the amount of data that can be 
sent. Cat6 has a higher bandwidth compared to Cat5. 
 
CAT5 can support up to 10/100 Mbps at a 100MHz bandwidth. However, a newer 
version of CAT5 cable in the market, CAT5e, can support up to 100/1000 Mbps

idth. However, a newer 
version of CAT5 cable in the market, CAT5e, can support up to 100/1000 Mbps at a 
350MHz bandwidth. In comparison, CAT6 can support up to 1000 Mbps (1GbE) at a 
250MHz bandwidth. Better yet, a newer version of CAT6 that Cablesys offers can 
support up to 1GbE at a 500MHz bandwidth.

Types of Bridges:  
Th

? What is its significance? What are different types of bridges? 
  
 
9 
 
 
Types of Bridges:  
There are three types of bridges in computer networks, which are as follows: 
 Transparent bridge 
 Source routing bridge 
 Translational bridge 
 
Transparent Bridge: 
Transparent bridges are invisible to other devices on the network. This bridge 
doesn’t reconfigure the network on the addition or deletion of any station. The prime 
function of the transparent bridge is to block or forward the d

deletion of any station. The prime 
function of the transparent bridge is to block or forward the data according to the 
MAC address.  
 
Source Routing Bridge:  
Source routing bridges were developed and designed by IBM specifically for token 
ring networks. The frame’s entire route is embedded with the data frames by the 
source station to perform the routing operation so that once the frame is forwarded 
it must follow a specific defined path/route.  
 
Translational Bridge: 
Translational b

forwarded 
it must follow a specific defined path/route.  
 
Translational Bridge: 
Translational bridges convert the received data from one networking system to 
another. Or it is used to communicate or transmit data between two different types 
of networking systems. Like if we are sending data from a token ring to an Ethernet 
cable, the translational cable will be used to connect both the networking system 
and transmit data.

18. Why RTS CTS is used in 802.11? 
 
RTS/CTS (Request to Send / Clear to Send) mechanism is a reservation scheme 
used in the wireless networks. It is used to minimize frame collisions created due to 
the hidden node problem.

19. Explain CSMA/CD protocol 
It is a carrier sense multiple access based on media access protocol to sense the 
traffic on a channel (idle or busy) before transmitting the data. It means that if the 
channel is idle,

fic on a channel (idle or busy) before transmitting the data. It means that if the 
channel is idle, the station can send data to the channel. Otherwise, it must wait 
until the channel becomes idle 
 
  
 
20. Explain CSMA/CA protocol 
Carrier sense multiple access/collision avoidance (CSMA/CA) is a protocol for 
carrier transmission in 802.11 networks. It was developed to minimize the 
potential of a collision occurring when two or more stations send their signals over 
a data link layer

22. What is WAN? 
WAN is Wide Area Network, It is a collection of local-area networks (LANs) or other 
networks that communicate with one another. A WAN is essentially a network of 
networks, with the Internet the world's largest WAN.

Internet is the world's largest WAN 
 
24. When Internet came in India? 
Internet in India began in 1986 and was initially available only to the educational 
and research community. General public access to the internet in India began on 15 
August 1995.

30. What is subnetting? What is need of subnetting? 
 
Subnetting is the process of creating a subnetwork (also known as a subnet) w

need of subnetting? 
 
Subnetting is the process of creating a subnetwork (also known as a subnet) within 
a network. 
 
One goal of a subnet is to split a large network into a grouping of smaller, 
interconnected networks to help minimize traffic and improve security. 
14 
 
 
 
  
 
Example:  
Divide the network 192.168.5.0 into 4 sub networks 
 
The given network is from Class C. Hence, 24 bits are for network and 8 bits are 
there for host. Hence its default subnet mask is 255.255.255.0 
 
N

are for network and 8 bits are 
there for host. Hence its default subnet mask is 255.255.255.0 
 
Now to form 4 sub networks, we need 2 bits from host into network. 
 
 
31. What is supernetting? 
 
Supernetting is a technique in computer networking for combining multiple 
smaller network addresses into a single larger network address. Doing it allows 
more efficient routing and reduces the size of the routing table. The reason for 
supernetting is to improve the efficiency of routing in large-

of the routing table. The reason for 
supernetting is to improve the efficiency of routing in large-scale networks. 
 
It is also called as address aggregation. 
  
32. What is subnet mask? 
 A subnet mask is a 32-bit number created by setting host bits to all 0s and 
setting network bits to all 1s.  
 In this way, the subnet mask separates the IP address into the network and 
host addresses.

40. Why there is need to make different classes of IP? 
An IP address class is needed to provide a way to divide IP addresses into groups. 
This is important because it makes it easier to manage and organize them. 
 
IP Address class was developed keeping in mind different classification of users of IP 
addresses. One class of users need

veloped keeping in mind different classification of users of IP

such users (like government organizations) , for such users class A is there. 
Similarly for users with up to 65536 IP address need they created class B. Class C is 
for user who need small amount of IP addresses (not more than 256), majority of 
users fall in this category. Class D & E were reserved for special purposes.

44. What is ARP and RARP? 
Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is a protocol or procedure that connects an ever-
changing Internet Protocol (IP) address to a fixed physical machine address, also 
known as a media access control (MAC) address, in a local-area network (LAN). 
 
19 
 
  
In Reverse ARP (RARP ) MAC address is mapped to IP address.

47. What is difference between Link state routing and distance vector routing? 
 
Distance vector routing  
 It is a dynamic routing algorithm in which each router computes a distance 
between itself and each possible destination i.e. its immediate neighbors. 
 The router shares its knowledge about the whole network to its neighbors and 
accordingly updates the table based on its neighbors. 
 The sharing of informat

its neighbors and 
accordingly updates the table based on its neighbors. 
 The sharing of information with the neighbors takes place at regular intervals. 
  
21 
 
Link state routing  
 It is a dynamic routing algorithm in which each router shares knowledge of its 
neighbors with every other router in the network. 
 A router sends its information about its neighbors only to all the routers 
through flooding. 
 Information sharing takes place only whenever there is a change 
 
48. List vari

hrough flooding. 
 Information sharing takes place only whenever there is a change

50. Explain different socket calls? 
  
 
 
23 
 
 socket() creates an endpoint for communication and returns a file descriptor that refers 
to that endpoint 
 
The bind() system call associates an address (port number and IP add

hat refers 
to that endpoint 
 
The bind() system call associates an address (port number and IP address) with the 
socket descriptor. 
 
The listen() system call prepares a connection-oriented server to accept client 
connections. 
 
The connect() system call connects the socket referred to by the file descriptor sockfd to 
the address specified by addr 
 
The accept() system call initiates communications (3-way handshake) between a 
connection-oriented server and the client.

DNS, or the Domain Name System, translates human readable domain names (for 
example, www.amazon.com) to machine readable IP addresses (for example, 
192.0.2.44) 
 
 DNS Records: 10 types of records

machine readable IP addresses (for example, 
192.0.2.44)

DNS Servers: 3 types of DNS servers 
 
DNS Root Server: 
The authoritative name servers that serve the DNS root zone, commonly known as the 
“root servers”, are a network of hundreds of servers in many countries around the world. 
They are configured in the DNS root zone as 13 named authorities, as follows 
 
27 
 
  
Top Level Domain Server (TLD S

in the DNS root zone as 13 named authorities, as follows 
 
27 
 
  
Top Level Domain Server (TLD Server) 
In the DNS hierarchy, a top-level domain (TLD) represents the first stop after the root 
zone. In simpler terms, a TLD is everything that follows the final dot of a domain name. 
For example, in the domain name ‘google.com’, ‘.com’ is the TLD. Some other popular 
TLDs include ‘.org’, ‘.uk’, and ‘.edu’. 
 
The Root Zone Database represents the delegation details of top-level domains, includ

and ‘.edu’. 
 
The Root Zone Database represents the delegation details of top-level domains, including 
gTLDs such as .com, and country-code TLDs such as .uk. As the manager of the DNS 
root zone, IANA (Internet Assigned Numbers Authority) is responsible for coordinating 
these delegations in accordance with their policies and procedures. 
 
Authoritative Name Server 
The authoritative nameserver is usually the resolver’s last step in the journey for an IP 
address. The authoritative nameserver

is usually the resolver’s last step in the journey for an IP 
address. The authoritative nameserver contains information specific to the domain name 
it serves (e.g. google.com)

76. What is the difference between collision domain and broadcast domain 
 
Collision occurs at Data Link Layer while Broadcasting happens at Network layer. 
 
A collision domain is a network segment connected by a shared medium or through 
repeaters or hubs where simultaneous data transmissions collide with one another. 
The collision domain applies particularly in wireless networks, but also affected early

another. 
The collision domain applies particularly in wireless networks, but also affected early 
versions of Ethernet. 
 
Collision domain can be reduced by using switches. 
 
38 
 
A broadcast domain is a collection of devices that receive broadcast traffic from each 
other. Broadcast domain can be reduced by subnetting.
